coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125 for efficient photocatalytic production of H2O2 under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Jianhui
Pu, Kaikai
Zhao, Ting
Shi, Jiating
Zheng, Na
Nie, Lijun
Xue, Kunkun
Gao, Yuhang
coredata.dc:description: NH2-MIL-125 and its derivatives are receiving more attention in various aspects of photocatalytic reactions, especially in the photocatalytic hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production from water (H2O) and oxygen (O2), which is a promising and sustainable strategy. However, the generation of H2O2 from NH2-MIL-125 is far from satisfactory due to rapid photo-generated carriers recombination and poor surface electron transfer. In the work, the composite photocatalyst CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125 (C/T/NM) was designed for the first time by one-step hydrothermal method. TiO2 was in situ converted from partial NH2-MIL-125 (NM) during the successful loaded of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by hydrothermal process. The results indicated the type Ⅱ heterojunction was successfully constructed between the NM and TiO2 interface, which could promote the transmission of photo-generated electrons. In addition, the successful loaded of CQDs could effectively transfer and stored the photo-generated electrons to the photocatalyst surface to participate in the reaction, and further avoiding the recombination of photo-generated carriers. The C/T/NM composite photocatalyst achieved a H2O2 generation of 455 µmol/L for 5 hours under visible light without oxygen bubbling, which was 7.1 times superior to that of NM. The H2O2 generation rate reached 645.4 µM/(g·h), which was in priority in the reported literature under the same conditions. Finally, based on the active species capture experiments, energy band structure analysis and the photoelectrochemical measurements, a possible mechanism for the efficient H2O2 generation through C/T/NM had been proposed. This work provided new ideas for designing NH2-MIL-125 based composite photocatalysts for the production of H2O2.

Type Ⅱ heterojunction
Photocatalytic hydrogen peroxide production

head.ce:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125 for efficient photocatalytic production of H2O2 under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030600, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: NH2-MIL-125 and its derivatives are receiving more attention in various aspects of photocatalytic reactions, especially in the photocatalytic hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) production from water (H2O) and oxygen (O2), which is a promising and sustainable strategy. However, the generation of H2O2 from NH2-MIL-125 is far from satisfactory due to rapid photo-generated carriers recombination and poor surface electron transfer. In the work, the composite photocatalyst CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125 (C/T/NM) was designed for the first time by one-step hydrothermal method. TiO2 was in situ converted from partial NH2-MIL-125 (NM) during the successful loaded of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by hydrothermal process. The results indicated the type Ⅱ heterojunction was successfully constructed between the NM and TiO2 interface, which could promote the transmission of photo-generated electrons. In addition, the successful loaded of CQDs could effectively transfer and stored the photo-generated electrons to the photocatalyst surface to participate in the reaction, and further avoiding the recombination of photo-generated carriers. The C/T/NM composite photocatalyst achieved a H2O2 generation of 455 µmol/L for 5 hours under visible light without oxygen bubbling, which was 7.1 times superior to that of NM. The H2O2 generation rate reached 645.4 µM/(g·h), which was in priority in the reported literature under the same conditions. Finally, based on the active species capture experiments, energy band structure analysis and the photoelectrochemical measurements, a possible mechanism for the efficient H2O2 generation through C/T/NM had been proposed. This work provided new ideas for designing NH2-MIL-125 based composite photocatalysts for the production of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:para: The increasing demand for sustainable and clean energy have driven research on alternatives to fossil fuels with carbon-free energy sources (Pei et al., 2023; Shi et al., 2023). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is one of the 100 most essential chemicals with widespread applications in organic synthesis (Gao et al., 2020), disinfection (Chen et al., 2023; Fan et al., 2022), wastewater treatment (Wu et al., 2022) and the pulp industry (Machado et al., 2000) at a rate of 2.2 million tons per year. It is worth noting that consumption of H2O2 has increased dramatically with the outbreak of the various new viruses Epidemic (Shen et al., 2023). However, current industrial H2O2 production mainly relies on the conventional anthraquinone process, which can cause severe environmental pollution and requires the usage of noble metal catalysts (Ghosh et al., 2023; Kim et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2023a). At present, the electrocatalytic and photocatalytic H2O2 production is considered a sustainable alternative to the traditional anthraquinone process (Li et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2021a; Zhao et al., 2020a). Compared with serious energy consumption in the electrocatalytic method, the photocatalysis is a energy saving, environmentally friendly and sustainable method and it can capture sunlight and drive semiconductors to produce H2O2 under mild conditions (Pan et al., 2022; Xue et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023a).

Generally, the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is the most widely investigated pathway for photocatalytic generation of H2O2 (Che et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2023). In the ORR pathway, photogenerated electrons directly react with oxygen and the photogenerated holes react with electron donors to provide some oxygen and protons, ultimately producing H2O2. In addition, the ORR reaction pathways for H2O2 generation could be further classified as one-step two-electrons reaction (Eq. (1)) and two-step one-electron reaction (Eqs. (2) and (3)) (Luo et al., 2023a; Zeng et al., 2021). The reaction potential of the one-step two-electrons ORR pathway (+0.68 V) is more positive than that of the two-step one-electron ORR pathway (-0.33 V), indicating that the one-step two-electrons ORR is thermodynamically more favourable. On the contrary, the two-step one-electron ORR pathway is only one electron required for each step, indicating it is more advantageous in terms of reaction kinetics (Chen et al., 2021a). Therefore, based on the reaction process, the photocatalytic generation of H2O2 is mainly determined by excellent optical absorption, a suitable conduction band position, ideal photogenerated carriers separation, surface electron transfer and adsorbed oxygen on the surface of photocatalyst (Gu et al., 2022; Zhou et al., 2023).
(1) O2+2H++2e−→H2O2
(2) O2+e−→O2 −
(3) O2 −+2H++e−→H2O2

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are constructed from interconnected metal ions and multifunctional organic ligands, and they have received widespread attention due to their diverse structure and functionalities, abundant active sites, large specific surface area, and easy modification (Feng et al., 2022; Xu et al., 2023a; Zhang et al., 2021b). While NH2-MIL-125, a typical Ti-based MOF material, has been demonstrated to be a promising and efficient solar energy conversion photocatalyst owing to its unique photocatalytic properties (Muelas-Ramos et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020a), easy modification (Shah et al., 2021) and large surface area (Zhao et al., 2021). However, the limited visible light responsive and rapid recombination of photo-generated carriers significantly limit the efficiency of NH2-MIL-125 in photocatalytic production of H2O2 under visible light (Ji et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023). Therefore, a series of efficient strategies are developed to enhance the efficiency of NH2-MIL-125 photocatalytic production of H2O2. For instance, Chen et al. synthesized 4,4,4,4-(pyrene-1,3,6,8-tetrayl) tetrabenzoic acid (L2)-functionalized MIL-125 with more structural defects of missing ligands and more efficient visible light responsive ability, which allowed it to efficiently produce H2O2 through O2 reduction (Chen et al., 2021b). Jiang et al. (2023) successfully constructed MIL-125/WO3-x S-scheme heterojunction, which could improved charge separation and transfer, and effectively enhance the photocatalytic production of H2O2 through the oxidation H2O pathway. Liu et al. (2022) constructed a heterostructure of NH2-MIL-125@ZnS, and the structure was controlled by adjusting the ZIF-8/ZnS diameter, which controlled the generation of electron-hole pairs, charge separation and stability of the photocatalyst, ultimately improved the performance of photocatalytic generation of H2O2.Thus, constructing heterojunctions is an effective strategy to overcome the limitations of NH2-MIL-125 and further improve its photocatalytic performance in producing H2O2 (Dong et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2023b).

Titanium dioxide (TiO2), as a traditional semiconductor catalyst, has also been widely studied based on its good biocompatibility, chemical stability, low cost and low toxicity (Dai et al., 2022; Keerthana et al., 2022; Mohammadi-Aghdam et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2022a). In addition, the synthesis of TiO2 using NH2-MIL-125 as a precursor is receiving widespread attention, which allow TiO2 to inherit its large specific surface area, more active sites, and expand its visible light absorption (Huang et al., 2022; Ke et al., 2022; Song et al., 2022a; Zhang et al., 2023b; Zhao et al., 2020b). It should be noted that the conduction band minimum (CBmin) and valence band maximum (VBmax) of TiO2 match well with the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of NH2-MIL-125 for type I or type II heterojunction (Jiao et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). Therefore, there had been reports of designing and synthesizing heterojunction photocatalytic materials composed of TiO2 and NH2-MIL125 by controlling the loss ratio of organic ligands using the “solvent post thermal path” (Wang et al., 2022) and “in-situ acid etching strategy” methods (Xu et al., 2023b). However, using water as an etchant to partially convert NH2-MIL-125 into TiO2 in situ to synthesize TiO2/NH2-MMIL-125 composite materials is more environmentally friendly than solvent and acid etching.

Although the construction of heterojunction between TiO2 and NH2-MIL-125 can effectively reduce the rapid recombination of photo-generated carriers and poor structural stability, the poor migration and utilization of photo-generated electrons as well as the finite visible light responsive ability still greatly limit the performance of highly efficient photocatalytic H2O2 production. To overcome the above problems, loading carbon quantum dots (CQDs) on the surface of TiO2 and NH2-MIL-125 heterojunction materials through a simple hydrothermal reaction seems to be an effective solution. According to literature reports, CQDs are spherical carbon nanomaterials, which possess the advantages of low toxicity, easy functionalization, low cost, excellent upconversion fluorescence properties, and electron storage capacity (Fang et al., 2023; Zheng et al., 2022). Therefore, loading CQDs on heterojunction composite materials can greatly improve their photocatalytic H2O2 production performance, which is mainly reflected in two aspects. Firstly, due to the upconversion luminescence property of CQDs, it can convert near-infrared light into visible light, greatly improving the light utilization efficiency of heterojunction composite photocatalytic materials. Secondly, CQDs anchored on the surface of heterojunction composite photocatalytic materials can migrate and store photo-generated electrons, which can further enhance the separation of photo-generated electrons-holes and stabilize photo-generated electrons, thereby improving the effective reaction between electrons and oxygen (Wang et al., 2019).

Based on the above analysis, The CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125 (C/T/NM) composite photocatalyst with in-situ generation of TiO2 and loading of CQDs on the surface of NH2-MIL-125 was synthesized for the first time through a one-step hydrothermal method. The specific process is shown in Scheme 1 . In the one-step hydrothermal process, TiO2 was partially transformed from NH2-MIL-125 in situ to form TiO2/NH2-MMIL-125 due to the loss of organic ligands, at the same time, CQDs was also successfully loaded on its surface. A series of characterization results confirmed that the type Ⅱ heterojunction was successfully constructed between the NH2-MIL-125 and TiO2 due to the well-matched band structure and close-contact interface between the two materials. Moreover, TiO2 in situ converted from NH2-MIL-125 provided anchoring sites for loaded of CQDs. The successful constructed heterojunction could efficiently promote the separation of photogenerated carriers and the transmission of photo-generated electrons, and induce photogenerated electron into CQDs of the surface of CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125. Furthermore, the CQDs could capture and store the photogenerated electrons on the surface of CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125, increasing the reaction opportunity between electrons and oxygen. In addition, CQDs possessed unique optical properties, which greatly improved the optical absorption of C/T/NM, including light absorption capacity and light response range. Overall, the efficient photo-generated electron transfer pathway had been successfully constructed on the CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125 composite photocatalysts, and achieving a high H2O2 yield without bubbling oxygen under visible light. Additionally, the possible mechanism of efficiency H2O2 generation through CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125 under visible light was also explored. This work provided a new insight into the design of photocatalytic production of H2O2 under visible light based on NH2-MIL-125.

Methanol (MeOH, 99.7%) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥ 99.5%) were purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co. Butyl titanate (TBT, 97%), isopropanol (IPA, 99.7%), silver nitrate(AgNO3), ammonium oxalate ((NH4)2C2O4), benzoquinone (BQ, 98%) and AgNO3 were purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory, China. Amino terephthalic acid (NH2-BDC, 98%) and melamine were purchased from Shanghai MacLean Biochemical Technology Co., China. Glucose (C6H12O6, 99.7%) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.7%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Hydrochloric acid (HCl, 98%) and glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH, 99.5%) were purchased from Tianjin Chemical Reagent Wholesale Company. Ammonia (NH3·H2O), concentrated sulfuric acid (purity 98%) and hydrogen peroxide (purity 30%) were purchased from Sinopharm.

NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) was synthesized according to the reported solvothermal method in the literature (Zhang et al., 2021c). Specifically, 27 mL of DMF and 3 mL of MeOH were mixed in a beaker, and then 1.36 g of 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC) was added to the mixed solution for sonication until a clear solution was formed. Then 0.51 mL of butyl titanate was added to the clear solution for sonication for 30 min before transferring to an 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacting at 150°C for 48 h. The collected samples were washed several times with DMF and methanol and then dried overnight in an oven at 60 °C. The obtained NH2-MIL-125(Ti) was designated as NM.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared using the modified alkali-assisted sonication method (Feng et al., 2020). Specifically, 9.0 g of glucose was uniformly dissolved in 50 ml of ultra-pure water, and then 50 mL of ammonia was added and continuously stirred for 8 h. Afterward, the colorless mixture solution was sonicated for 120 min at room temperature to obtain a brown raw solution. The raw solution was adjusted to pH = 7 by adding HCl and dialyzed through a semipermeable membrane. Finally, a transparent brown solution was obtained and referred to as the CQDs aqueous solution for later use.

A composite photocatalyst with in-situ generation of TiO2 and loading of CQDs on the surface of NH2-MIL-125 was synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method. In detail, 1.0 g NH2-MIL-125 and 20 mL CQDs solution were added to 40 mL ultra-pure water and stirred at room temperature for 6 h. Afterwards, the mixture was transferred to an 80 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor and reacted at 180 °C for 8 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting product was washed three times with water and ethanol, dried in an oven at 80 °C. The final collected sample was CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125, named C/T/NM.

For a comparative study, TiO2 formed by the complete conversion of NH2-MIL-125 under the same synthesis conditions as C/T/NM was prepared. Only the CQDs solution was replaced with a 20 mL mixture of ammonia and HCl at pH = 7 and the resulting sample was collected as hydrothermal titanium dioxide (designated as h-TiO2).

A 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cut-off filter was used to provide the visible light. There was no oxygen exposure in the reaction system. Specifically, 0.075 g of photocatalyst was added to 180 mL of ultra-pure water, and then 20 mL of isopropanol was added as a sacrificial agent. Before the photocatalytic H2O2 production reaction, a dark reaction of 30 min was carried out to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and oxygen. During the reaction, 5 mL suspension was taken from the reaction system at regular intervals and filtered to remove the photocatalyst. The amount of H2O2 was analyzed by potassium oxalate titanium method (Zhang et al., 2010). In detail, 5 mL of potassium titanium oxalate, 5 mL of reaction mixture and 0.65 mL of 98% concentrated sulfuric acid (through 0.22 µm filter membrane) were transferred into a 25 mL colorimetric tube with a pipette and it was supplemented to the scale mark. The generated H2O2 could react with potassium titanium oxalate in acidic medium to form a stable orange complex. Then the absorbance of the complex was measured at 400 nm and the concentration of H2O2 was determined by comparing the standard curve. In addition, under the same conditions as H2O2 production reaction, the decomposition kinetics of H2O2 was studied by adding the photocatalyst to the H2O2 solution with an initial concentration of 0.1 mmol/L.

The crystal phases of the photocatalysts were analyzed on an X-ray diffractometer (D8-FOCUS, Bruker, Germany) with Cu Ka radiation (k = 1.5418 Å). The microstructures and surface morphology were investigated through transmission electrons microscopy (TEM, JEM2100 FS, JEOL, Japan), scanning electrons microscopy (SEM, JSM-7900F, JEOL, Japan) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS, JSM-6701F instrument, JEOL, Japan). The chemical compositions and states were measured by a X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB Xi+, Thermo Scientific, USA). Chemical groups and chemical bonds were studied using a FT-IR spectrophotometer (IR Tracer 100, Shimadzu, Japan) with BaSO4 as the reference standard. Optical absorption property was tested using Shimadzu UV-Visible diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (DRS, Lambda 1050, PerkinElmer, USA) with BaSO4 powder as the reflectance standard. Steady and transient photoluminescence (PL) spectra was recorded on a fluorescence lifetime spectrophotometer (FLS920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with a 150 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Photocurrent response spectroscopy, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and Mott-Schottky tests were all conducted on the CHI660E workstation (Chenhua Instrument, Shanghai, China) using a standard three electrode system, in which a 300 W Xe lamp was used as the light source and 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte solution. The sample (active area of 1 cm2) coated on ITO was used as the working electrode. Hg/Hg2-Cl2 electrode and Pt electrode were used as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to investigate the morphologies of NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM. The SEM image of NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM are illustrated in Fig. 1 (a-d). The results in Fig. 1(a) showed that NM presented a decahedral structure with a smooth surface and a particle size of approximately 700 nm, which corresponded to the morphology reported in the published literature (Cheng et al., 2021; Li et al., 2021a). The SEM image of h-TiO2 as shown in Fig. 1(b) displayed irregular shaped aggregates with a particle size of around 50-100 nm, which resulted from the complete hydrolysis of organic linker (Dimethyl aminoterephthalate, NH2-BDC) in NM under the hydrothermal conditions (Chen et al., 2020). The SEM image in Fig. 1(c-d) exhibited that C/T/NM had a decahedral structure, indicating that it still maintained a relatively complete NM metal skeleton structure. Unlike NM, C/T/NM had a rough surface due to the presence of TiO2 particle aggregates attached to its surface. The TiO2 particle aggregates were converted from partial NM during the loading of CQDs. Furthermore, X-ray energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping in Fig. 1(e-i) of C/T/NM revealed the uniform distribution of C, Ti, O, and N elements in C/T/NM, further confirming the successful synthesis of C/T/NM.

In the C/T/NM composite photocatalyst, the presence of CQDs could not be clearly observed in the SEM results due to the small size of the CQDs. Therefore TEM characterization of C/T/NM was carried out. As shown in Fig. 1(j), TiO2 particle aggregates transformed from NM were dispersed on the surface of C/T/NM. The irregular aggregates with size of 50-100 nm were circled by the orange line in Figure, which was consistent with the results of the SEM analysis. Furthermore, the CQDs particles with a size of 5-10 nm were uniformly dispersed and deposited on the surface of C/T/NM (Wang et al., 2019), and the shaded area as marked by the red circle was the CQDs particles, which was indicated successfully loaded of CQDs. It was worth emphasizing that CQDs were deposited in the region of formed TiO2, which proved that TiO2 provided rich anchoring points for the deposition of CQDs. Fig. 1(k) was an enlarged view of the area marked by dotted line in Fig. 1(j), which clearly showed two different lattice fringes. The lattice spacing of 0.35 nm corresponded to the (101) crystal plane of anatase TiO2 (Mahato et al., 2021), and the lattice spacing of 0.197 nm corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs (Song et al., 2022b). This also provided the evidence of the successful loading of CQDs on the surface of C/T/NM. Furthermore, as circled by the blue line in Fig. 1(k), a large proportion of the CQDs lattice stripes appeared to overlap with lattice stripes of TiO2, once again confirming that TiO2 provided the basis for the successful loading of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of sample structure and composition
ce:section.ce:para: XRD patterns of the prepared samples are shown in Fig. 2 (a), the diffraction peaks of NM at 2θ = 6.7°, 9.7°, 11.6°, 15.2° corresponded to its (101), (002) and (211) crystal planes, respectively, and the diffraction peaks at 2θ=15.2°, 16.6°, 17.9°, 19.5° and 21.5° was also corresponded to its (222), (132), (004), (042) and (233) crystal planes, respectively (Abdelhameed et al., 2018; He et al., 2021), and the characteristic diffraction peaks of NM is the same as the simulated NH2-MIL-125 characteristic diffraction peak, which was indicated the successful preparation of NM. For the h-TiO2 sample, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 25.3°, 37.8°, 48.1°, 55.1°, and 62.7° corresponded to the (101), (004), (200), (211), and (204) crystal planes of anatase-type TiO2 (PDF: #21-1272) (Kang et al., 2022), while all of the characteristic diffraction peaks of NM disappeared, demonstrating NM was completely transformed into anatase TiO2 by hydrolysis of the linker NH2-BDC without the addition of CQDs solution. Meanwhile, due to the addition of CQD solution in the system, only a portion of NM was transformed into TiO2 under hydrothermal conditions, while some NM still maintained their original structure. This was because the CQDs covered on C/T/NM prevent further hydrolysis of NM. The obtained results were consistent with the observed morphological changes described in the SEM images.

To further investigated the main factors involved in the conversion of NM to anatase TiO2 during the synthesis process of C/T/NM. The XRD results of C/T/NM obtained at different hydrothermal temperatures of 60, 90, 150, 180, and 200°C (hydrothermal time was 8 h) and different hydrothermal time of 2, 4, 8, and 10 h (hydrothermal temperature was 180°C) are shown in Fig. 2(b) and (c). The results indicated that as the hydrothermal temperature increased, the characteristic diffraction peak intensity of NM gradually weakened, while the characteristic diffraction peak intensity of TiO2 gradually increased. However, NM showed partial transformation to TiO2 at different hydrothermal time (180°C), but the characteristic diffraction peak intensities of NM and TiO2 remained almost unchanged with the change of hydrothermal time. The above results revealed that the transformation of NM to anatase TiO2 mainly depended on the hydrothermal temperature.

The functional groups and molecular structures of the prepared samples were analized through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) to determine their chemical composition. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the prepared NM showed the characteristic stretching vibration absorption bonds of Ti-O-Ti in titanium-oxygen clusters of 400-800 cm−1. The absorption peaks at 1252 cm−1, 1434 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 were belonged to the C-N stretching vibration peak, the -OH symmetric stretching vibration peak and the N-H bond of NH2-BDC, respectively. The peaks at 1504 and 1552 cm−1 were gave because of the -COO asymmetric stretching vibrations (Zhang et al., 2024). Additionally, the absorption broad peaks between 3345-3440 cm−1 were attributed to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of amine (Li et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2022b). For h-TiO2, the broad peak of h-TiO2 at 400-800 cm−1 was attributed to the O-Ti-O bond of the anatase phase TiO2 (Zhang and Pan, 2011), which was significantly different from the characteristic vibrational absorption band of the titanium oxygen cluster in NM. Moreover, the characteristic peaks of NH2-BDC were also found to exist in the infrared spectrum of h-TiO2, indicating that the h-TiO2 formed by NM conversion still retained some of the NH2-BDC structure. For C/T/NM, the stretching vibration peaks of Ti-O clusters, C-N, -NH2, and COO− same as NM were still retained, but their intensity was significantly weakened. At the same time, a broad peak similar to h-TiO2 also appeared in the range of 400-800 cm−1. In addition, the absorption peak at 1732 cm−1 and the absorption band at 3100-3400 cm−1 was observed in the FT-IR plots of C/T/NM, which were attributed to the stretching vibration peak of C=O and hydroxyl groups (-OH) of CQDs (Lu et al., 2016; Zhao and Zhao, 2018). Based on the above analysis of C/T/NM, a portion of NM was converted in situ to TiO2, and the CQDs was successfully loaded on the surface of C/T/NM.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were used to analyse the elemental composition and surface chemical states of NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM. Fig. 3 (a) shows the XPS survey spectra of the three samples, which all displayed C 1s, N 1s, Ti 2p, and O 1s peaks, indicating the existence of C, N, Ti, and O elements in each sample. Compared with NM, the intensity of the C 1s and N 1s peaks showed a significant decrease in the full XPS spectra of h-TiO2, which was attributed to the loss of some organic ligands during hydrothermal process of converting NM to h-TiO2. Differently, the full XPS spectra of C/T/NM showed an increase in the intensity of the C 1s peak and decrease in the Ti 2p and O 1s peak compared with NM. This was because CQDs were successfully loaded onto the surface of C/T/NM, and some NM was in situ converted to TiO2. Fig. 3(b) shows that the C 1s energy spectrum of the three samples, comparing with the C 1s energy spectrum of NM, it was observed that the intensity of C=C peak and COO− peak in the C 1s energy spectrum of h-TiO2 and C/T/NM was obviously weakened, which might be attributed to the loss of some organic ligands. While the COO− peak and C=C peak at 288.7 eV and 286.6 eV showed a shift towards the low binding energy, which might be attributed to the breakage of the coordination bond between the Ti-O clusters and the NH2-BDC during the hydrothermal process. The further displacement of the COO− peak in the C 1s energy spectrum of C/T/NM was mainly due to the strong electronic interaction between TiO2 and NM for C/T/NM composite. Moreover, the C-N peak with binding energy at 285.8 eV of NM shifted to lower binding energy positions in h-TiO2 and C/T/NM, which might be attributed to the electronic interaction between TiO2 and NM caused by the TiO2 attached to the amino functional group. In Fig. 3(c), for N 1s XPS spectrum, the two peaks at 399.5 eV and 401.6 eV in NM could be correlated with the -NH2 group of NH2-BDC in NM and the outward extension or protrusion of the N element in the -NH2 group into the cavity of NM, respectively (Huang et al., 2019; Low et al., 2009). Compared to the N 1s energy spectra of the NM, the disappearance of the -N+ peak at 401.6 eV and the appearance of new peak at 400.9 eV of h-TiO2 and a shift (0.3 eV) of the new peak toward a lower binding energy compared to h-TiO2 in the energy spectrum of C/T/NM was observed, respectively. The disappearance of the -N+ peak of h-TiO2 and the displacement of -N+ peak in C/T/NM was attributed to the Ti-O-N bond formed due to interaction between NH2-BDC and TiO2 (Zhang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2018), which confirmed the disruption of the part of NM crystal structure and partial TiO2 formed in-situ from NM conversion in C/T/NM after hydrothermal process. In Fig. 3(d), there were three typical peaks at 529.9 eV, 531.6 eV and 532.7 eV in three samples, which were attributed to Ti-O, C=O, and C-OH, respectively. The C-OH peak attributed to the titanium oxygen cluster in the O 1s energy spectrum of h-TiO2 almost disappeared compared with NM, which was due to the conversion of the titanium oxygen cluster in the structure to TiO2. In addition, a significant increase in the relative content of Ti-O in h-TiO2 compared to NM and C/T/NM, which were mainly derived from the complete or partial conversion of the anatase phase TiO2. And the weak displacement of C=O peak in h-TiO2 and C/T/NM compared with NM again illustrated the interaction between the organic ligand NH2-BDC of NM and the TiO2 converted form NM. Compared to the O 1s energy spectrum of NM, the increase of C-OH peak at 532.7eV and C=O peak at 531.1 eV in C/T/NM was derived from the hydroxyl functional groups and the carbon-containing function groups on the surface of the loaded CQDs (Yin et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021c). Fig. 3(e) shows the Ti 2p energy spectra of NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM. Two peaks at 458.7 eV and 464.4 eV in NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM correspond to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively, confirming the presence of Ti4+ in the titanium oxygen cluster. Moreover, the two binding energies of Ti shifted to lower positions of 458.5 eV and 464.2 eV for h-TiO2 and C/T/NM, which might be attributed to the conversion of Ti from NM to TiO2.

Totally, according to XRD, FT-IR and XPS measurements, we successfully prepared C/T/NM by one-step hydrothermal process, which retained the NM structure and was partially converted to TiO2, accompanied by the successful loading of CQDs, while some TiO2 interacted with the amino groups on the organic linker of NM. In addition, the composition ratio of NM and TiO2 in C/T/NM was successfully controlled by changing the temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of energy band structure of samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 (a) displays the UV-visible diffuse reflectance spectra of NM, h-TiO2, and C/T/NM. The light absorption edge of NM was approximately 500 nm and corresponded to the band gap of 2.74 eV by Tauc plots (Appendix A Fig. S1). In addition, the visible light absorption range of C/T/NM was further enhanced, even approaching the NIR region due to the excellent up-conversion fluorescence characteristics of CQDs (Huang et al., 2021). Both C/T/NM/ and h-TiO2 had a good light absorption capacity, which was consistent with their color (Ma et al., 2024). However, the band gap of C/T/NM and h-TiO2 could not be accurately estimated through linear fitting based on DRS results due to the black absorption. Therefore, to determine the accurate energy band structure of the samples, XPS valence band spectra and Mott-Schottky plots were investigated. The XPS valence band spectra of NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM are shown in Fig. 4(b), and the measured valence band maximaum (VB) were 2.23, 2.36 and 2.35 eV, respectively. We also tested the Mott-Schottky curves of three samples at 800 Hz, 1000 Hz and 1200 Hz (Appendix A Fig. S2). Fig. 4(c) presents the Mott-Schottky plots of three samples at 1000 Hz. The potentials measured were converted to reversible hydrogen electrode (vs RHE) based on the formula (Eq. (4)) (Li et al., 2024; Song et al., 2021), and the flat band potentials (vs. RHE) were determined to be -0.51, -0.43, and -0.55 V for NM, h-TiO2, and C/T/NM, respectively. The positive slopes confirmed the n-type semiconductor features of the samples, and the minimum conduction band (CB) of n-type semiconductors was negative 0-0.1 V compared to the flat charged potential (Wang et al., 2016). The CB positions of NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM were determined equal to the flat band potentials to be -0.51, -0.43 and -0.55 V (vs. RHE), respectively. Combined with the Mott-Schottky curve and the XPS valence band spectrum, the band gap (Eg) of NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM could be calculated by E g = E VB - E CB to be 2.74, 2.89 and 2.90 eV, respectively.

where, E ° Ag/AgCl is the correction factor for Ag/AgCl with respect to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), and equals 0.197 V at 20 °C, pH = 3.5.

Based on the above results, the energy band structure diagrams of NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM are illustrated in Fig. 4(d). Obviously, the VB and CB potentials of NM and h-TiO2 were a well matched, indicating the two had the conditions to construct a type II heterojunction for the formation of C/T/NM. Furthermore, compared to the VB and CB potentials of heterojunction formed between NM and h-TiO2, the CB potential of C/T/NM was positively shifted to -0.55 V, which was more favourable for photocatalytic H2O2 production by ORR reaction. This was due to the fact that successful loaded of CQDs could shift the CB potential positively (Fang et al., 2023). Moreover, both one-step two-electrons pathway (Eq. (5)), two-step one-electron pathway (Eqs. (6) and (7)) and water oxidation pathway (Eq. (8)) were thermodynamically feasible for C/T/NM. This would be further demonstrated in visible light photocatalytic production of H2O2 and active species capture experiments.
(5) O2+2e−+2H+→H2O2 (E = 0.68V vs. NHE)
(6) O2+e−→O2 − (E = -0.33V vs. NHE)
(7) O2 −+e−+2H+→H2O2 (E = +1.44V vs. NHE)
(8) 2H2O+h+→H2O2+2H+ (E = +1.76V vs. NHE)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficient photocatalytic H2O2 production of C/T/NM
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic H2O2 production efficiency was studied for C/T/NM synthesized under the different conditions (loading amount of CQDs, hydrothermal synthesis temperature and time) under visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the photocatalytic H2O2 generation performance of C/T/NM was gradually improved with the increased of CQDs loading. When the loading amount of CQDs was 20 mL, the photocatalytic H2O2 generation reached the maximum, which was 0.184 mmol/L. However, as the loading amount of CQDs continued to increase, the generation of H2O2 was decreased. This was due to the fact that excessive amount of CQDs would cover the surface of the C/T/NM, and hindering the photocatalyst active sites, resulting in a decrease of its photocatalytic activity (Xu et al., 2023b). As illustrated in Fig. 5(b), with the increase of hydrothermal synthesis temperature of C/T/NM, TiO2 was gradually converted from NM and CQDs were successfully loaded on the surface of C/T/NM, which led the photocatalytic H2O2 generation performance of C/T/NM was gradually improved. When the hydrothermal synthesis temperature was increased to 180°C, the H2O2 concentration of C/T/NM was obviously increased to 0.184 mmol/L for 5 h and the H2O2 concentration was decreased when the hydrothermal synthesis temperature was 200°C. Similar to the effect of hydrothermal time on the structural composition of C/T/NM, the H2O2 concentration of C/T/NM was obviously increased when the hydrothermal time was 8 h (Appendix A Fig. S3). This was because the appropriate hydrothermal synthesis temperature and hydrothermal time could successfully form a heterojunction interface between TiO2 and NM, which maximized the photocatalytic activity of C/T/NM for H2O2 production. While excessive hydrothermal synthesis temperature and time would cause too much loss of organic linkers of NM, which led to the destruction of the organic framework structure (Wang and Yu, 2022; Xu et al., 2023b), thus resulting in the decrease of the photocatalytic H2O2 generation activity.

The effects of the dosage of the C/T/NM composite photocatalyst, the initial pH of the reaction solution, the types of electron donors and the concentration of electron donors were further investigated as single variables on its photocatalytic activity of H2O2 production, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the effect results of the initial solution pH of 3-11 on the photocatalytic H2O2 generation of C/T/NM were explored. The best H2O2 generation activity was achieved at pH = 7 and the H2O2 concentration of C/T/NM was decreased under the acidic and alkaline conditions, respectively. It was worth noting that the photocatalytic H2O2 production activity relied on the surface electrons and protons transfer on the photocatalyst. However, in acidic solutions, the excess H+ would react with H2O2 (H2O2+H++e−→H2O+OH− and H2O2+2H++2e−→2H2O (Zeng et al., 2021)), resulting in the decomposition of part H2O2. Moreover, when the solution was under the alkaline conditions, the OH− present in the solution would consume H+, which would reduce the efficiency of the photocatalytic H2O2 generation. At the same time, the generated H2O2 would also further decompose with a higher concentration of OH−. In addition, the H2O2 concentration was significantly decreased to 0.15 mmol/L and 0.13 mmol/L in extreme environments at pH = 3 and pH = 11, respectively. This was also due to the structure of NM was unstable at the extreme pH conditions and the protonation of the terephthalate linkers resulted in demetallation of the linker from Ti8O8(OH)4 clusters and thus destroyed the structure of NM (Jiang et al., 2021). The dosage of photocatalyst could also affect its photocatalytic activity for producing H2O2. As shown in Appendix A Fig. S4, the amount of H2O2 produced by C/T/NM photocatalyst increased sequentially with the increase of its dosage, and the H2O2 production efficiency tended to be stable at a dosage of 0.075 g. Considering the utilization of the photocatalyst and the H2O2 yield, the optimal dosage of the C/T/NM was 0.075g. This was because excessive photocatalyst could shield the absorption and utilization of visible light. In addition, different alcohols were added as electron donors to the reaction system, and their effects on the photocatalytic production of H2O2 by C/T/NM are analyzed in Fig. 5(d). The results showed that the addition of isopropanol as the electron donor was more conducive to generation of H2O2 than ethanol and methanol, which was mainly due to the fact that the same volume of isopropanol could provide more H+ to participate in the reaction of H2O2 generation of C/T/NM (Luo et al., 2023b; Zhang et al., 2020b). Therefore, the effect of the amount of added isopropanol on the activity of C/T/NM photocatalytic production of H2O2 was also discussed in Appendix A Fig. S5. The results showed that the amount of H2O2 produced by C/T/NM increased as the amount of isopropanol increased. When the amount of isopropanol was 20 mL, the maximum concentration of produced H2O2 was 0.321 mmol/L, which could be regarded as the equilibrium point for reaction and mass transfer. Further increase in IPA amount led to an increase in system viscosity, causing a sharp slowdown in oxygen diffusion rate and reaction deceleration, resulting in a decrease of the concentration of H2O2 (Zhang et al., 2020b). Therefore, C/T/NM had the optimal photocatalytic H2O2 production performance at the loading amount of CQDs, hydrothermal synthesis temperature and time was 20 mL, 180°C and 8 h, respectively and the reaction system was pH=7, the dosage of C/T/NM was 0.075 g, and 10 vol% isopropanol of 20 mL was added. The subsequent C/T/NM photocatalytic production of H2O2 experiments were conducted under the optimal experimental conditions mentioned above.

The photocatalytic H2O2 production activity of NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM was evaluated without bubbling oxygen under visible light irradiation (300W xenon light source, λ>420 nm) using IPA as the electron donor. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), the H2O2 production concentration of NM for 5h was only 0.064 mmol/L, and the H2O2 production concentration of h-TiO2 was 0.049 mmol/L, which was slightly lower than that of NM. This was because the positions of the valence and conduction bands of NM and h-TiO2 resulted in insufficient driving force for their photocatalytic production of H2O2. The rapid recombination of photo-generated carriers was also the main reason for their low efficiency in photocatalytic production of H2O2. Remarkably, compared with NM and h-TiO2, the H2O2 concentration produced by C/T/NM reached a high level of 0.455 mmol/L, which was about 7.1 and 9.3 times higher than that of NM and h-TiO2, respectively. The H2O2 concentration was also at a preferred level compared with other photocatalysts reported in the literature under the same conditions (Appendix A Table S1), with the highest rate of H2O2 generation up to 645.4 µM/(h·g). The result was mainly attributed to the excellent visible light capture ability and the efficient separation and migration efficiency of photo-generated carriers based on the creation of a heterojunction between TiO2 and NM in C/T/NM. Moreover, CQDs served as highly effective electron-trapping and storing materials (Feng et al., 2020) inhibited the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole and the photo-generated electrons accumulated in the CQDs on the surface of C/T/NM would react more effectively with oxygen molecules adsorbed its surface to form H2O2 through ORR pathway.

It should be noted that the accumulated H2O2 concentration was determined by the dynamic equilibrium of H2O2 generation and decomposition. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the decomposition experiment of H2O2 with initial concentration of 0.1 mmol/L by NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM was carried out. It could be clearly observed that the decomposition ability of photocatalysts towards H2O2 gradually weakened after 2 h. The H2O2 decomposition during the reaction process mainly came from the further reaction of photo-generated electron-hole with H2O2 (Zeng et al., 2021) (Eqs. (9) and (10)), whereas the decomposition efficiency of H2O2 by NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM were 11.8%, 13.4%, and 14.5% after 5 h, respectively. The C/T/NM showed the the highest H2O2 decomposition efficiency, which was due to its excellent photo-generated carriers separation and migration ability, allowing more electrons and holes to participate in the H2O2 decomposition.
(9) H2O2 + e− → OH + OH−
(10) H2O2 + h+ → O2 − + 2H+

Therefore, the formation rate constants (K f) and decomposition rate constants (K d) were fitted according to the following kinetic model (Eq. (11)) (Wu et al., 2020):

In this model, the formation rate followed zero-order kinetics, while the decomposition rate followed first-order kinetics. As illustrated in Fig. 6(c), the rate constant (K f) of C/T/NM (242 µM/h) was 8.3 and 12.1 times higher than those of NM (29 µM/h) and h-TiO2 (20 µM/h), respectively, which was in agreement with their photocatalytic H2O2 production performance. However, C/T/NM had a higher decomposition rate constant (K d, 0.485 h−1) compared to NM and h-TiO2, but it was only 1.3 and 1.5 times greater than those of NM (0.388 h−1) and h-TiO2 (0.329 h−1), respectively. It could be observed that the successful loaded of CQDs improved the migration and utilization of photo-generated electrons, which were predominantly utilized for combining with O2 to generate H2O2 rather than decomposing H2O2.

The reusability and stability of photocatalysts were essential properties for practical applications and the cyclic stability of C/T/NM was investigated in this study. As shown in Fig. 6(d), the photocatalytic production efficiency of H2O2 by C/T/NM only decreased by 17% after five cycles of experiments. While the photocatalytic H2O2 production of h-TiO2 and NM decreased by 35% and 39% after five cycles of experiments, respectively (Appendix A Fig. S6). Furthermore, the FT-IR and XRD pattern of C/T/NM (Appendix A Fig. S7) after five cycles of experiments did not change significantly compared with the sample before reaction. Therefore, C/T/NM possessed excellent cycling and structural stability, which could be attributed to the fact that the structure of C/T/NM could be protected by external TiO2 (Li et al., 2021b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanisms of C/T/NM photocatalytic production of H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further explore the cause and reaction mechanism of efficient photocatalytic H2O2 production for C/T/NM, the migration and separation performance of photo-generated carriers of samples was characterized, and the active species and reaction pathways of photocatalytic H2O2 production was also evaluated. The transient photocurrent response of NM, h-TiO2, and C/T/NM was recorded for several on-off cycles. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the photocurrent density increased drastically for C/T/NM compared with NM and h-TiO2, confirming that C/T/NM had a more efficient charge separation and faster electron transfer performance. Moreover, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test also demonstrated similar results in Fig. 7(b), where the C/T/NM with the smallest radius had the smallest electron transfer resistance, i.e., the smoothest photo-generated electron migration path. The excellent photogenerated carriers separation (Li et al., 2020) and transfer performance of C/T/NM was attributed to the formation of heterostructure with well matched valence and conduction band structures at the interface of C/T/NM. Moreover, the loaded CQDs could promote efficient transfer of photo-generated electrons to the surface of C/T/NM. To further confirm the above findings, the steady-state photoluminescence spectra (PL) was used to study the recombination of photo-generated electrons-hole at the excitation wavelength of 240 nm (Fig. 7(c)). All samples showed the intrinsic emission peaks of NM and TiO2 at a wavelength of 483 nm, which was formed by the transition of photo-generated electrons from the bottom of the conduction band to the top of the valence band of photocatalyst (Hong et al., 2013). The lower emission peak intensity indicated better separation of photo-generated electrons-holes. (Xue et al., 2023). It could be clearly seen that C/T/NM exhibited the lower emission peak intensity than that of h-TiO2 and NM, which was due to a heterojumction structure was successfully constructed between the NM and TiO2 interface and CQDs were also successfully loaded on the surface of C/T/NM. The results were consistent with the analysis of transient photocurrent and EIS test, and the effective photo-generated electron-hole separation ability and the surface electron transfer ability of C/T/NM was confirmed again, which could promote the reaction between photo-generated electrons and oxygen to generate H2O2.

To explore the possible reaction mechanism of C/T/NM composite photocatalyst for efficient H2O2 production under visible light, the active species were evaluated through active species capture experiments. As shown in Fig. 7(d), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 0.5 mmol/L), p-benzoquinone (BQ, 0.1 mmol/L), ammonium oxalate ((NH4)2C2O4, 0.04 mmol/L), and isopropanol (IPA, 0.13 mmol/L) were used as photo-generated electrons (e−), superoxide radical (·O2 −), hole (h+) and hydroxyl radical (·OH) trapping agents, respectively. In detail, the concentration of H2O2 produced by C/T/NM did not change significantly when IPA was added. It was because the valence band position of the C/T/NM composite photocatalyst was about +2.35 V, which was lower than the electrochemical potential of the WOR pathway for the production of H2O2 via ·OH (H2O + h+ → ·OH + H+ E = + 2.73 V vs. NHE) (Zeng et al., 2021). As a result, ·OH was not the main intermediate species in the H2O2 formation reaction. The H2O2 concentration slight decreased when (NH4)2C2O4 was added, indicating that only a small amount of H2O oxidized by h+ resulting in the formation of H2O2 (2H2O+2h+→H2O2+2H+). However, the H2O2 concentration decreased sharply in the presence of AgNO3, which confirmed that photo-generated electrons were the main participants in the generation of H2O2. When BQ was added, the generation of H2O2 was also significantly inhibited, but there was still a certain amount of H2O2, which first indicated that ·O2 − was the primary intermediate species for the generation of H2O2 and that the reaction path was a sequential two-step single-electron reaction (O2 + e− → ·O2 −, ·O2 + e− + 2H+ → H2O2), which also illustrated that there was a few H2O2 generated by one-step two-electrons reaction pathway (O2 + 2e −+ 2H+ → H2O2). Furthermore, it also implied that the efficient separation and migration of photogenerated electrons of C/T/NM greatly enhanced the photocatalytic H2O2 generation of C/T/NM under visible light.

As shown in Fig. 7(e), for NM and h-TiO2, the change trend of the generated H2O2 by them was similar to that of C/T/NM after capturing h+ and ·OH. After capturing ·O2 −, the H2O2 generation of NM, h-TiO2, and C/T/NM decreased by 53.1%, 53.0%, and 52.3%, respectively. This result implied that the proportion of H2O2 generated by two-step single-electron pathway for C/T/NM was highly consistent with the change trend of the H2O2 generated proportion for NM and h-TiO2. In addition, the H2O2 generation by NM, h-TiO2 and C/T/NM decreased by 63.7%, 48.6% and 74.1%, respectively when AgNO3 was used to capture e−, indicating that the photocatalytic production of H2O2 by C/T/NM mainly depended on the reaction of e− with adsorbed O2, and C/T/NM exhibited a higher proportion through a one-step two-electrons reaction to generate H2O2 compared to NM and h-TiO2. It also further demonstrated that C/T/NM presented excellent photocatalytic H2O2 production activity was due to the CQDs acted as an electron trapping material to capture photo-generated electrons and the type Ⅱ heterojunction constructed between the NM and TiO2 interface of C/T/NM. Moreover, the concentration of ·O2 − in the reaction system was also quantitatively detected. As shown in Fig. 7(f), the concentration of ·O2 − first increased and then decreased with the extension of illumination for 5 h, which was highly consistent with the change trend of the H2O2 generation efficiency for C/T/NM. This result meant that the two-step single-electron reaction was the main generation path of H2O2. In summary, for C/T/NM, the photocatalytic H2O2 production was mainly though two-step single-electron reaction, one-step two-electrons reaction and a small amount of h+ oxidation of H2O, and the first one was the primary reaction pathway.

Based on the above analysis, Fig. 8 illustrates the possible mechanism of photocatalytic production of H2O2 under visible light by C/T/NM. Due to the proper energy level configuration and the close contact with NM and TiO2, type Ⅱ heterojunction in the C/T/NM was formed. In addition, the uniformly loaded of CQDs on C/T/NM could enhance the migration and storage of photo-generated electrons on the surface of C/T/NM. Consequently, the photocatalytic H2O2 generation of C/T/NM under visible light was significantly increased. When λ > 420 nm light irradiated the composite photocatalyst, it directly promoted the separation of photo-generated electrons-holes in C/T/NM. The potential of the valence band of h-TiO2 (2.46 eV vs. RHE) was more positive than the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) potential of NM (2.10 eV vs. RHE), which caused the photo-generated holes to migrate towards the HOMO of NM. A small portion of the photo-generated holes oxidized water to produce H+ and H2O2 (path 1), but most of the holes were consumed by isopropanol to generate large amounts of H+. On the contrary, the photo-generated electrons transferred from the organic ligands of NM to the titanium oxide cluster to form Ti3+, and then the photo-generated electrons donated by Ti3+ were transferred from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of NM (-0.51 eV vs. RHE) to the conduction band of TiO2 (-0.44 eV vs. RHE). Meanwhile, CQDs loaded on the surface of C/T/NM acted as an electron trapping material to capture and stabilize photo-generated electrons. In this process, the photo-generated electrons and the oxygen adsorbed by C/T/NM directly generated H2O2 through a one-step two-electron reaction pathway (Path 2), and more photo-generated electrons generated H2O2 through a two-step one-electron reaction pathway (Path 3). In addition, the photo-generated electrons migration channel from NM to h-TiO2 and subsequently to the CQDs on C/T/NM has been constructed successfully, which could effectively inhibit electrons backflow and recombination with holes, thus greatly promoting the photocatalytic generation of H2O2 under visible light.

In summary, we designed and synthesized the composite photocatalyst CQDs/TiO2/NH2-MIL-125 (C/T/NM) by one-step hydrothermal method. The TiO2 was in situ converted from partial NM during the successful loaded of CQDs by hydrothermal process. Furthermore, the formation of various ratios TiO2 on the surface of NM was controlled through adjusting the hydrothermal temperature and time. The SEM, TEM, EDS, FT-IR, and XRD results confirmed that the morphology of C/T/NM and spatial relationships of TiO2, NM and CQDs as well as the successful loaded of CQDs. The results of DRS, XPS-VB, and Mott-Schottky showed that the valence and conduction band positions of TiO2 and NM were well matched and successful in situ constructed a type II heterojunction on surface of C/T/NM, which could efficiently promote the separation of photogenerated carriers. Moreover, the C/T/NM showed a remarkably better visible light response and a more favourable energy band structure for H2O2 generation through the ORR pathway reaction due to the successful loading of CQDs. Furthermore, PL, EIS, and transient photocurrents analyses had illustrated possessed good separation and migration efficiency of photo-generated holes and electrons. Upon further analysis through active species capture experiments and the ·O2 − quantitative capture test, the possible reaction mechanism of C/T/NM composite photocatalyst for efficient H2O2 production under visible light was explored. The the generation of H2O2 pathway for C/T/NM included the two-step single-electron pathway reaction, the one-step two-electrons pathway reaction, as well as a very small part of the h+ oxidative H2O reaction, and the first path was the main reaction. As a result, C/T/NM presented excellent photocatalytic H2O2 production activity, and its concentration of H2O2 under visible light for 5h reached 0.455 mmol/L, which was about 7.1 and 9.3 times higher than that of NM and h-TiO2, respectively. The formation rate of H2O2 for C/T/NM (645.4 µM/(g ·h)) was also in priority in reported literature under the same conditions.
